fields.definition_origin: Term coined by Jakob Nielsen (Nielsen Norman Group, 2006 — 'Progressive Disclosure'); foundational concept in instructional design dating to John Carroll's minimalist instruction work in the 1980s. NN/g defines it as: show only the few most-important options up front; expose secondary options on demand. Echoed in IBM Carbon, Material Design, Apple HIG, and Refactoring UI (Wathan/Schoger 2018). Microsoft's Larry Tesler also articulated the principle as 'less is more' for Newton OS.
fields.core_mechanism: Working memory holds 3-7 items (Sweller 1988 cognitive load theory; Miller 1956 magic number 7±2). Single-screen 'show everything' interfaces exceed this capacity, triggering anchor formation around perceived complexity (PMC12956955 cohort study) that persists regardless of later utility. Progressive disclosure splits the cognitive load across screens — each screen has one decision, working memory holds context across screens via the state machine, and the user perceives 'a few simple steps' rather than 'a complex system'. Pairs with Goal Gradient Effect (Hull 1932; Kivetz 2006): chunked progress visible at each step compounds motivation toward completion.
fields.neuroscience_correlate: Reduces extraneous cognitive load on dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (Sweller 1988 framework operationalized via fMRI in van Merriënboer & Sweller 2005). Lower working-memory occupancy frees germane load for schema acquisition — the user actually learns the app rather than burning cycles parsing the screen. [uncertain on direct mobile-onboarding fMRI]
fields.empirical_evidence: (1) NN/g eyetracking studies show simpler initial screens reduce 'visual fixation count' by ~30-40% on the first scan. (2) Sweller's cognitive load research (1988+) is the foundational citation — replicated 100+ times in instructional contexts (van Merriënboer & Sweller 2005 meta-review). (3) PMC12956955 (2024, N1=100, N2=87, N3=80) showed initial complexity perception persists even when later utility is high — implies front-loaded simplicity is critical. (4) Mobbin/Page Flows teardowns of YouTube Android (canonical contextual progressive disclosure), Linear, Notion all show signature progressive layered onboarding. (5) Adapty 2026 paywall report: top-performing apps disclose price details progressively (start with outcome, expose price last) — one of the most consistent A/B winners. (6) PDF anchor study cited in outline: opening 7-10 seconds set persistent perception.
fields.measurable_outcome_metric: Step completion rate (target: ≥85% per step, ≥60% end-to-end for 5-7 step flows; ≥40% end-to-end for 20+ step flows), time-on-step (target: 8-15s per step for questionnaire, longer for reveal), abandonment-by-step funnel curve shape (should be a gentle slope, not a cliff at any one step).
fields.conversion_lift_benchmark: NN/g case studies show 20-40% lift in completion when single-screen forms split into 3-5 progressive-disclosure steps. PDF source cites multi-step bars yielding 'materially higher' completion vs single-page. Specific lift varies by category — wellness/habit: +25-30%, productivity: +10-15%.
fields.engineering_tactics: (1) State machine (XState v5) with one screen per state; entry actions render the screen, exit actions persist context. (2) Single primary CTA per screen — never two competing actions. (3) Lazy-load secondary screens / detail panels behind explicit user expansion (e.g., 'Advanced settings' accordion). (4) Recursive application: account → personalization → permissions → paywall → empty state — each layer only shown after prior completes. (5) Server-driven UI optional — if iteration speed matters, render screens from JSON sequence (item 31). (6) Animation as cue — slide-from-right on forward, slide-to-right on back, communicates linearity. (7) Progress indicator (item 29) — show step N of M with endowed-progress (start at 2 of 8 not 1 of 7). (8) Skip / 'Tell me more' affordance for advanced users on optional steps. (9) Code-level: prefer composition (`<Screen step=2 />`) over conditional rendering (`{step===2 && <Step2/>}`) for testability and analytics instrumentation.
fields.real_world_examples: (1) YouTube Android contextual coach marks — only fire when user navigates to an unfamiliar surface (Library, Studio), not on cold launch (canonical NN/g example). (2) Linear onboarding — 4 steps: workspace name → invite → first issue → done. Each step single-purpose. (3) Notion onboarding — workspace → templates → first page → done; templates/customization disclosed later in-app. (4) Cal AI 33-step questionnaire — ONE question per screen (extreme progressive disclosure, paired with sunk-cost). (5) Stripe Atlas onboarding — chunked into 'company info', 'founders', 'banking', 'review' sub-flows with visible section progress. (6) Things 3 first launch — empty Today list with single 'Magic Plus' affordance disclosed; advanced features (Areas, Projects, Tags) revealed contextually as user adds tasks. (7) Headspace 2024 onboarding — interest → goal → timing → reminder, four sequential single-question screens.
fields.mobile_vs_web_diffs: Mobile mandate: thumb-zone layout (primary CTA in bottom 1/3 of screen), one-screen-one-decision rule because viewport is constrained. Web: more permissive — desktops can hold 2-3 disclosure tiers per page (sidebar + main + detail). Web equivalent: accordion-based settings (Stripe Dashboard), tab-based feature reveal (Notion sidebar), 'Show more' progressive content (Twitter timeline). Mobile motion (slide transition) signals progression more strongly than web (page navigation).
fields.platform_design_language_alignment: Strong alignment with both Liquid Glass (iOS 26+) and Material 3 Expressive (Android 16+). Liquid Glass's bolder left-aligned alert/onboarding typography (WWDC25) is purpose-built for single-question-per-screen patterns. M3E's expressive shape system supports clearer step hierarchy via card / sheet primitives. Both DLs have ChainAnimator-equivalent for sequential reveal; use platform-native page transitions (UIPageViewController, ViewPager2) over custom slides.
fields.category_fit: Universal — works in every category. Strongest fit: wellness/habit (Cal AI 33-step extreme), fintech (Acorns disclosure of bank-link → goals → first deposit), B2B/productivity (Linear, Notion). Acceptable fit: utilities with simple flows (3-step welcome). Weakness: when value prop is universal AND single-feature, four cinematic screens (item 25) outperform progressive disclosure.
fields.tooling_options: XState v5 (state machine), React Navigation Stack (RN), SwiftUI NavigationStack, Jetpack Compose Navigation, Plotline / Appcues Mobile / Pendo / Product Fruits (managed onboarding), OnboardJS, Storyly for story-format disclosure. Server-driven: Lovi protocol-oriented approach.
fields.ai_dependency: none: progressive disclosure is a pattern, not an AI capability. Reveal screens that are LLM-personalized (item 12) live INSIDE the disclosure structure; the disclosure pattern itself works without AI.
fields.claude_design_application: Instruct Claude Design to model the flow as an FSM and emit one component per state. Prompt fragment: 'Build an onboarding flow as an XState v5 machine. Output `OnboardingMachine` with states: welcome → goal → painpoint → metrics → reveal → paywall. Each state renders ONE Screen component with ONE primary action and ONE secondary action max. Show progress bar at top: "Step N of 5" starting at "Step 2 of 6" (endowed progress). Use `slide_from_right` page transition (cubic-bezier(0.32, 0.72, 0, 1) at 280ms). Forbid: rendering more than one decision per screen; rendering all six screens in a single scrollable view; using a Lucide chevron.'
fields.stitch_application: Prompt fragment: 'Mockup a 5-screen mobile onboarding sequence at 390×844 (iPhone 15). Each screen: top has a progress bar (filled to step N/5, animated width). Body: ONE headline (32px Cabinet Grotesk Bold, left-aligned, ≤45 chars), ONE supporting paragraph (17px Manrope Regular, ≤2 lines), ONE input (single question or 4 chip-style options). Bottom: primary CTA (full-width, 56px tall, bottom inset 24px, brand accent color), and a small "Skip" tertiary text-link above the CTA. Forbid: more than one question per screen; multiple CTAs of equal weight; centered headlines.'
fields.ai_codegen_failure_mode: AI codegen defaults to either (a) a single-screen mega-form with all fields visible (data-collection mindset), (b) a deck-of-cards horizontal carousel that taxes working memory equivalently, or (c) a tab-bar 'wizard' that hides progress. Counter-prompt: 'Output ONE component per step in a state-machine architecture. Each step renders ONE primary decision. Forbid: fields rendered in scrolling list on a single screen; horizontal carousel of feature-tour cards; tab-based step navigation. Show explicit progress indicator at top with endowed-progress start (Step 2 of 6 not Step 1 of 5).'
fields.anti_patterns: (1) Wall-of-Text single-screen onboarding (Six-Antipattern Audit #1). (2) Deck-of-cards 'tour' carousel masquerading as progressive disclosure — same total cognitive load, just paginated. (3) Hiding REQUIRED data behind 'Advanced' toggles (creates re-onboarding when user hits the requirement later). (4) Disclosing items in arbitrary rather than dependency-ordered sequence (asking for goals before identity). (5) Progress bar that doesn't reflect actual progress (jumping from 50% to 90% on the easy steps).
fields.regulatory_risk: Low. Progressive disclosure aligns with GDPR Article 5(1)(b) purpose limitation (collect what's needed when needed) and Apple Guideline 5.1.1(v) data minimization. EU Digital Fairness Act (Q4 2026 draft) likely views progressive disclosure favorably as the antithesis of dark-pattern overwhelm. Edge case: if 'progressive disclosure' is used to hide subscription terms behind multiple taps (FTC Click-to-Cancel violation), risk escalates to High.
fields.platform_constraints_2026: Apple Guideline 5.1.1(v) data minimization explicitly favors progressive collection. Apple Guideline 3.1.2 (toggle-paywall ban effective 2026-01) means subscription terms must be visible up-front on the paywall — progressive disclosure CANNOT hide pricing details behind taps. Android: Play Data Safety form requires declaration of data types collected; progressive collection still requires the same disclosure.
fields.accessibility_implications: Progressive disclosure improves screen-reader UX (one focused screen at a time). WCAG 2.2 alignment: 2.4.3 Focus Order (single screen = clear order), 3.2.3 Consistent Navigation (FSM transitions are predictable), 2.5.5 Target Size (one big CTA is easier than many small ones). Risk: disclosure transitions must respect prefers-reduced-motion (replace slide with crossfade). VoiceOver focus must move to the new screen's first heading on transition (use `accessibilityElementsHidden` on outgoing screen, `UIAccessibility.post(notification:argument:)` to focus new heading).
fields.pitfalls_caveats: (1) Long flows can feel 'are we there yet' — pair with progress indicator (item 29) and chunked sub-progress for 20+ step flows. (2) Back navigation must restore state (XState persist + MMKV) — losing entered data is a fatal trust break. (3) Each screen adds latency on slow networks if state is server-mirrored — pre-fetch the next screen's data. (4) Step count creep — every PM wants one more step; lock the count via design-doc.
fields.cross_cultural_validity: Universally applicable. NN/g cites cross-cultural research showing progressive disclosure helps users in all language contexts (text-density tolerance is roughly invariant). RTL: page transitions reverse direction (slide-from-left on forward in Arabic/Hebrew). CN super-app context: users tolerate denser screens but progressive disclosure still reduces error rate. JP context: users expect explicit completion confirmation between steps (add 'continue' microcopy).
fields.prescribed_action: Model the onboarding flow as a finite state machine with one screen per state and exactly one primary decision per screen. Sequence: account → personalization → permissions → paywall → empty state. Show a progress indicator with endowed-progress start (step 2 of N). Forbid single-screen mega-forms, horizontal feature-tour carousels, and any screen that asks two unrelated questions.
fields.questionnaire_relevance: Progressive disclosure IS the structural form of the questionnaire — one question per screen. Every question must clear working-memory thresholds (3-4 options max, single sentence prompt). Compounds with item 26 (sequencing) and item 28 (conversational vs form-style).
fields.aha_relevance: Progressive disclosure paces the user toward Aha — without it, the Aha screen drowns in noise. By stripping each screen to one decision, the eventual reveal screen (item 12) lands with maximum impact because the user has been trained to expect one signal per screen.
fields.emotional_archetype_alignment: Universal — every archetype benefits. Caregiver (gentle one-thing-at-a-time pacing), Sage (orderly progression), Magician (each step earns the reveal), Hero (linear journey toward goal). No archetype mismatch.
fields.anti_pattern_paired: Pairs against 'Wall-of-Text + Cognitive Overload' (Six-Antipattern #1). Also pairs against 'No-Escape Room' when combined with skip affordances and back navigation.
